title: Amorphous High Density Plutonium
authors: J. K. Katz, A. Rollett, R. J. Hemley
abstract: Metastable aluminum-alloyed $δ$-plutonium shrinks rapidly and pure $α$-plutonium swells rapidly at 4 K. At ambient temperature alloyed $δ$-plutonium swells about $10^{-3}$ as fast as it shrinks at 4 K, but its bulk density decreases more slowly than would be inferred from the increase in its lattice parameter determined by X-ray diffraction. These results might be explained as the result of ingrowth of the opposite phases, but they have not been found in X-ray diffraction. The cryogenic results may be explained by ingrowth of an amorphous phase with density intermediate between those of $α$ and $δ$. This phase, when formed from alloyed $δ$-plutonium, rapidly but not instantaneously anneals to $δ$-plutonium at temperatures $\gtrapprox 100\,$K; when formed from pure $α$-plutonium it anneals to $α$ at similar temperatures. The room temperature discrepancy between growth of lattice parameter and length of $δ$-plutonium is also explained by ingrowth of a denser amorphous phase that is continuously formed and annealed out.
summary: This paper investigates why plutonium undergoes rapid, unexpected swelling or shrinkage at very low temperatures under self-irradiation. The researchers found that the lattice expansion measured by X-ray diffraction does not match the bulk density changes, suggesting the creation of a new phase. They propose that radiation damage creates a high-density amorphous or glassy phase that acts as an intermediate state between the alpha and delta phases of plutonium. This discovery helps explain how the material recovers its original structure upon heating.
main_problem: Explaining the anomalous, rapid density changes and lattice parameter discrepancies in alpha and delta plutonium phases under self-irradiation at cryogenic and ambient temperatures.
main_result: The authors propose that radiation damage induces the formation of a metastable, high-density amorphous (glassy) phase with an intermediate density between the alpha and delta phases.
method: Experimental analysis using X-ray diffraction (XRD), dilatometry (bulk dimension measurements), and comparison with accelerated aging using 238-Pu spikes.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of plutonium phases, specifically high-density alpha-Pu and low-density, aluminum-alloyed metastable delta-Pu, subject to self-irradiation from alpha and beta decay.
key_observables: Lattice parameter (a), bulk density, linear dilation (length changes), and volumetric shrinkage/swelling rates.
important_parameters: Temperature regimes (4 K vs. room temperature), radiation dose (displacements per atom, dpa), and annealing temperature (~100 K).
main_assumptions: The assumption that accelerated aging via 238-Pu enrichment is equivalent to natural aging, and that the recrystallization of the amorphous phase is nucleated by the existing crystalline template.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares XRD data and dilatometry for Ga-stabilated delta-Pu, showing that the lattice constant increases more rapidly than the bulk sample dimension.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of anomalous density changes, presents experimental observations of cryogenic and ambient temperature behavior, rejects the phase conversion hypothesis in favor of an amorphous phase model, and discusses the implications for room-temperature dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
